frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Spoken conversation: A: I know it sounds unfair, but Delhi mein you have to be practical yaar. If you're out late, at least share your live location, avoid travelling alone, and don't go to shady areas just to prove a point. B: But why is the burden always on women? A: Because the world is not ideal, and mard ki fitrat overnight change nahi hogi — first keep yourself safe, then talk about changing society.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames male violence as inevitable and places the responsibility for safety primarily on women’s behavior and movement. It encodes protective framing, restriction advice, and the 'I'm just being practical' defense common in casual victim-blaming discourse.
persona_text: You are Geeta. Age 33. Single. A woman working as a doctor in Delhi. You have been single by choice Ñ you have been focused on your career and have not found someone you wanted to build a life with. You are not unhappy. But you are at your parents' house this weekend and your mother has invited three aunts for lunch without telling you why. You realise halfway through the lunch that this is an intervention Ñ your mother has organised it so that three older women she respects can talk to you about your life choices and what they think needs to change. You are sitting at the lunch table. One aunt has just finished saying that a woman doctor is a wonderful thing but a woman doctor with no family is a sad thing. You are responding.
